models.description: The Ferrari F430, produced from 2004 to 2009, features a naturally aspirated 4.3-litre V8 producing 483 bhp. Widely regarded as one of the finest mid-engine V8 Ferraris, well-maintained examples continue to hold strong value. Manual gearbox cars command a significant premium over F1 automated variants.

The Lamborghini Murciélago, produced from 2001 to 2010, is powered by a naturally aspirated V12 displacing 6.2 to 6.5 litres and producing 572 to 661 bhp. As the last Lamborghini flagship to offer a gated manual gearbox, well-maintained examples — particularly the LP640 and LP670-4 SV variants — are increasingly sought after by collectors. Manual gearbox cars command a significant premium over E-Gear automated variants.

The Ferrari 599 GTB Fiorano, produced from 2006 to 2012, is powered by a front-mounted 6.0-litre V12 producing 612 bhp. The flagship grand tourer succeeded the 575M Maranello and remains one of the most celebrated front-engine V12 Ferraris. The later 599 GTO variant, limited to 599 units, commands a significant premium.

The Ferrari 458 Italia, produced from 2009 to 2015, is powered by a naturally aspirated 4.5-litre V8 producing 562 bhp. Widely considered one of the greatest mid-engine Ferraris ever made, the 458 marked Ferrari's transition from the F1 automated manual to a dual-clutch gearbox. The track-focused Speciale variant is highly sought after by collectors.

The Ferrari 488 GTB, produced from 2015 to 2019, is powered by a twin-turbocharged 3.9-litre V8 producing 661 bhp. As Ferrari's first turbocharged mid-engine V8 since the F40, the 488 set new performance benchmarks. The Pista variant, with 710 bhp, is the most collectible version and commands a significant premium.

The Ferrari F12 Berlinetta, produced from 2012 to 2017, is powered by a front-mounted 6.3-litre V12 producing 730 bhp. As the most powerful naturally aspirated Ferrari road car of its era, the F12 succeeded the 599 GTB and delivered supercar performance in a grand tourer package. The limited-run F12tdf variant is exceptionally collectible.

The Ferrari 360 Modena, produced from 1999 to 2005, is powered by a naturally aspirated 3.6-litre V8 producing 394 bhp. The 360 introduced Ferrari's aluminium space-frame construction and represented a significant leap in design from its predecessor, the F355. Manual gearbox examples and the lightweight Challenge Stradale variant are the most sought-after by collectors.

The Lexus LFA, produced from 2010 to 2012, is powered by a hand-built 4.8-litre V10 producing 552 bhp, developed with Yamaha and revving to 9,000 rpm. Limited to just 500 units worldwide, the LFA is one of the rarest and most celebrated Japanese supercars ever built. The Nürburgring Package variant, limited to 50 units, is among the most valuable modern collectible cars.

The BMW M3, produced in various generations since 1986, is BMW's iconic high-performance compact sports car. From the E30's motorsport-bred four-cylinder to the E46's legendary inline-six, the E90/E92's screaming V8, and the F80/G80's twin-turbo six, each generation has defined its era. The E46 CSL, E92 GTS, and manual-gearbox examples are the most collectible variants.

The BMW M8, produced from 2019, is powered by a twin-turbocharged 4.4-litre V8 producing up to 625 bhp in Competition specification. Available as a Coupe, Convertible, and four-door Gran Coupe, the M8 represents BMW's flagship performance grand tourer. The Competition variant with its enhanced power output and sharper chassis tuning is the most desirable.

The Ferrari 166 Inter, produced from 1948 to 1950, was among the very first Ferrari road cars. Powered by a Colombo-designed 2.0-litre V12, it established the template for decades of Ferrari grand tourers to come.
The Ferrari 195 Inter, produced from 1950 to 1951, succeeded the 166 Inter with a larger 2.3-litre Colombo V12. A rare early Ferrari road car, it bridged the gap between the 166 and the 212.

The Ferrari 212 Inter, produced from 1951 to 1953, featured a 2.6-litre Colombo V12. Offered in both road and competition forms, the 212 helped establish Ferrari as a maker of desirable grand touring cars.

The Ferrari 342 America, produced from 1951 to 1953, featured a larger Lampredi-designed 4.1-litre V12. Aimed at the American market, only six examples were built, making it one of the rarest early Ferraris.

The Ferrari 250 Europa, produced from 1953 to 1954, was the first Ferrari to use the 3.0-litre Colombo V12 that would define the marque for decades. It laid the foundation for the legendary 250 GT series.

The Ferrari 375 America, produced from 1953 to 1955, was powered by a potent 4.5-litre Lampredi V12 derived from the Formula One engine. With only 12 examples built, it remains among the rarest and most valuable Ferraris.

The Ferrari 250 GT, produced in many forms from 1954 to 1964, is the defining Ferrari grand tourer. Powered by the legendary Colombo 3.0-litre V12, variants include the Berlinetta, Cabriolet, California, passo corto (SWB), 2+2, and the exquisite Berlinetta Lusso.

The Ferrari 410 Superamerica, produced from 1956 to 1959, was Ferrari's most powerful road car of its era. With a 5.0-litre Lampredi V12, it was the ultimate expression of Ferrari's grand touring philosophy in the 1950s.

The Ferrari 250 California, produced from 1957 to 1963, is one of the most beautiful and valuable Ferraris ever made. A convertible variant of the 250 GT, both long-wheelbase and short-wheelbase versions are among the most sought-after collector cars in the world.

The Ferrari 250 GTO, produced from 1962 to 1964, is widely considered the greatest Ferrari ever made. With only 36 examples built, the GTO is the most valuable car in the world, combining stunning Scaglietti bodywork with a race-bred 3.0-litre Colombo V12.

The Ferrari 400 Superamerica, produced from 1960 to 1964, succeeded the 410 SA with a Colombo-derived 4.0-litre V12. Available in both coupe and cabriolet forms with various coachbuilder bodies, only 47 were made.

The Ferrari 275 GTB, produced from 1964 to 1968, was a landmark Ferrari with independent rear suspension and a rear-mounted transaxle. The later GTB/4 variant with its four-cam engine is among the most desirable Ferraris of the 1960s.

The Ferrari 275 GTS, produced from 1964 to 1966, was the open-top counterpart to the 275 GTB. With Pininfarina bodywork and a 3.3-litre Colombo V12, the GTS offered refined grand touring in convertible form.

The Ferrari 330, produced from 1963 to 1968, was available as the GT 2+2, GTC coupe, and GTS spider. Powered by a 4.0-litre Colombo V12, the 330 family represented Ferrari's refined grand touring range of the mid-1960s.

The Ferrari 500 Superfast, produced from 1964 to 1966, was the most expensive and exclusive Ferrari of its era. With a 5.0-litre Colombo V12 producing 400 bhp, only 37 were built. It was the ultimate luxury Ferrari grand tourer.

The Ferrari 365 family, produced from 1966 to 1976, encompasses some of the most iconic Ferraris ever made. From the California spider to the legendary GTB/4 Daytona, the GT4 BB, and the comfortable GT4 2+2, the 365 range defined an era of Ferrari excellence.

The Ferrari Dino 206 GT, produced from 1968 to 1969, was the first mid-engined Ferrari road car. Powered by a 2.0-litre V6 with an aluminium body, only 152 were built before being replaced by the steel-bodied 246.

The Ferrari Dino 246, produced from 1969 to 1974, succeeded the 206 GT with a larger 2.4-litre V6 and steel body. Available as the GT coupe and GTS targa, the 246 is one of the most beautiful Ferraris ever made and highly sought after by collectors.

The Ferrari Dino 308 GT4, produced from 1973 to 1980, was the first production Ferrari with a V8 engine and 2+2 seating. Designed by Bertone rather than Pininfarina, it featured a mid-mounted 3.0-litre V8.

The Ferrari Dino 208 GT4, produced from 1975 to 1980, was a tax-friendly Italian-market variant of the 308 GT4 with a smaller 2.0-litre V8. Designed by Bertone, it shared the 308 GT4's Bertone-styled body.

The Ferrari 308, produced from 1975 to 1985, is one of the most recognisable Ferraris ever made. Available as GTB (coupe) and GTS (targa), later variants included fuel-injected GTBi/GTSi models and the Quattrovalvole four-valve versions.

The Ferrari 208, produced from 1980 to 1989, was an Italian-market model with a 2.0-litre V8 to comply with tax regulations. Available as GTB and GTS, with later turbocharged variants offering significantly more power than the naturally aspirated versions.

The Ferrari 400, produced from 1976 to 1985, was Ferrari's first car offered with an automatic transmission. A 2+2 grand tourer powered by a 4.8-litre V12, it was available as the Automatic and GT (manual), later updated to 400i with fuel injection.

The Ferrari 512 BB, produced from 1976 to 1984, succeeded the 365 GT4 BB with a larger 5.0-litre flat-12 engine. Available as the carburetted BB and fuel-injected BBi, plus the rare BB LM competition variant. The mid-engined flagship defined Ferrari's top-of-the-range sports car.

The Ferrari Mondial, produced from 1980 to 1993, was Ferrari's mid-engined 2+2. Evolving through Mondial 8, Quattrovalvole, 3.2, Cabriolet, and T versions, it offered four-seat practicality in a mid-engine package.

The Ferrari 288 GTO, produced from 1984 to 1987, was Ferrari's first turbocharged road car and the spiritual predecessor to the F40. Built to homologate for Group B racing, only 272 were made. Its twin-turbocharged 2.9-litre V8 made it one of the fastest cars of its era.

The Ferrari Testarossa, produced from 1984 to 1996, is one of the most iconic supercars ever made. Evolving through Testarossa, 512 TR, and F512 M variants, its side-strake design and flat-12 engine defined the supercar genre of the 1980s and 1990s.

The Ferrari 328, produced from 1985 to 1989, succeeded the 308 with a larger 3.2-litre V8 and refined styling. Available as GTB (coupe) and GTS (targa), it is widely regarded as one of the most reliable and accessible classic Ferraris.

The Ferrari 412, produced from 1985 to 1989, was the final evolution of the 400/400i series. A front-engined 2+2 grand tourer powered by a 5.0-litre V12, it was available with both manual and automatic transmissions.

The Ferrari F40, produced from 1987 to 1992, was the last Ferrari personally approved by Enzo Ferrari. Built to celebrate the company's 40th anniversary, its twin-turbocharged 2.9-litre V8, lightweight construction, and raw driving experience made it the definitive supercar of its era.

The Ferrari 348, produced from 1989 to 1995, succeeded the 328 with a longitudinally-mounted 3.4-litre V8. Available as TB/TS (testa-mounted belt), later updated to GTB/GTS and Spider variants with improved handling and refinement.

The Ferrari 456, produced from 1992 to 2003, was a front-engined V12 grand tourer. Available as GT (manual) and GTA (automatic), with a mid-life update to the 456M. The 456 offered effortless performance and long-distance comfort.

The Ferrari F355, produced from 1994 to 1999, is widely considered one of the finest mid-engine V8 Ferraris. Available as Berlinetta (coupe), GTS (targa), and Spider, the F355 combined a 3.5-litre V8 with sublime handling. Manual gearbox cars are particularly sought after.

The Ferrari F50, produced from 1995 to 1997, was a thinly-disguised Formula One car for the road. With a 4.7-litre V12 derived from the 1990 F1 engine, carbon-fibre construction, and only 349 built, the F50 is one of the most exclusive supercars ever made.

The Ferrari 550 Maranello, produced from 1996 to 2001, marked Ferrari's return to a front-mounted V12 berlinetta. Its 5.5-litre V12 and perfectly balanced chassis made it an instant classic. The limited Barchetta Pininfarina open-top variant is exceptionally collectible.

The Ferrari 575M Maranello, produced from 2002 to 2006, succeeded the 550 with a larger 5.7-litre V12 and available F1-style automated gearbox. A refined grand tourer with improved power and technology, manual gearbox cars command a premium.

The Ferrari Enzo, produced from 2002 to 2004, was named after the company's founder and represented the pinnacle of Ferrari road car technology. With a 6.0-litre V12 producing 651 bhp, Formula One-derived aerodynamics, and only 400 built, the Enzo remains one of the most celebrated supercars ever made.

The Ferrari 612 Scaglietti, produced from 2004 to 2011, was Ferrari's front-engined V12 grand tourer. Named after legendary coachbuilder Sergio Scaglietti, its aluminium body and 5.7-litre V12 delivered effortless performance in a luxurious 2+2 package.

The Ferrari FXX, produced from 2005 to 2007, was a track-only development of the Enzo. With a 6.3-litre V12 producing 789 bhp and only 30 built, the FXX was part of Ferrari's Corse Clienti programme. It represents the extreme of Ferrari's engineering capabilities.

The Ferrari 575 Superamerica, produced from 2005 to 2006, featured a unique electrochromic rotating roof that could transform from coupe to open-top in seconds. Based on the 575M, only 559 were built, making it a rare and innovative collector's piece.

The Ferrari California, produced from 2008 to 2017, was Ferrari's front-engined V8 grand tourer with a retractable hard top. Evolving through California, California 30, and the turbocharged California T, it combined open-top motoring with everyday usability.

The Ferrari FF, produced from 2011 to 2016, was Ferrari's first all-wheel-drive production car and its first four-seat grand tourer. With a 6.3-litre V12 producing 651 bhp and a unique shooting brake design, the FF was a revolutionary departure for Ferrari.

The Ferrari LaFerrari, produced from 2013 to 2018, was Ferrari's first hybrid hypercar. Combining a 6.3-litre V12 with an electric motor for a combined 950 bhp, only 500 coupes and 210 Aperta open-top versions were made.

The Ferrari GTC4Lusso, produced from 2016 to 2020, succeeded the FF as Ferrari's four-seat grand tourer. Available with a 6.3-litre V12 (GTC4Lusso) or twin-turbo 3.9-litre V8 (GTC4Lusso T), it combined everyday usability with Ferrari performance.

The Ferrari 812 Superfast, produced from 2017 to 2022, featured the most powerful naturally aspirated production V12 in history. Available as Superfast, GTS (retractable hard top), and the limited Competizione and Competizione Aperta track-focused variants.

The Ferrari Portofino, produced from 2018 to 2023, succeeded the California T as Ferrari's entry-level grand tourer with a retractable hard top. The updated Portofino M added an 8-speed gearbox and more power.

The Ferrari F8, produced from 2019 to 2022, was the final evolution of Ferrari's mid-engine twin-turbo V8 sports car lineage that began with the 488. Available as Tributo (coupe) and Spider, it delivered 710 bhp from its award-winning 3.9-litre V8.

The Ferrari Roma, produced from 2020, is a front-engined V8 grand tourer inspired by the carefree lifestyle of 1960s Rome. With elegant Pininfarina-influenced styling and a 3.9-litre twin-turbo V8, the Roma blends classic beauty with modern performance.

The Ferrari SF90 Stradale, produced from 2020, is Ferrari's plug-in hybrid supercar. Combining a 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 with three electric motors for a combined 986 bhp, it is the most powerful series-production Ferrari. Available as Stradale and Spider variants, plus the extreme XX versions.

The Ferrari Daytona SP3, produced from 2022, is part of Ferrari's Icona series paying homage to legendary racing prototypes. With a 6.5-litre V12 producing 829 bhp — the most powerful Ferrari internal combustion engine ever — and open-top targa bodywork, only 599 will be made.

The Ferrari Purosangue, produced from 2023, is Ferrari's first four-door, four-seat car. Powered by a naturally aspirated 6.5-litre V12 producing 715 bhp, it features suicide rear doors, all-wheel drive, and active suspension.

The Ferrari 296, produced from 2022, marks the return of a mid-rear-engined V6 to Ferrari's road car range. Its 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 hybrid produces up to 830 bhp. Available as GTB, GTS, and the track-focused Speciale variants.

The Ferrari F80, announced in 2024, is the most powerful Ferrari ever made. Combining a 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 with three electric motors for a combined 1,200 bhp, only 799 will be built. It succeeds the LaFerrari as Ferrari's flagship hypercar.

The Ferrari 12Cilindri, produced from 2024, is Ferrari's front-engined V12 grand tourer succeeding the 812. With a naturally aspirated 6.5-litre V12 producing 819 bhp, it is available as a coupe and Spider.

The Ferrari Monza SP1, produced from 2019, is a single-seat barchetta from Ferrari's Icona series. Powered by the most powerful Ferrari V12 at the time with 799 bhp, the SP1 pays homage to legendary Ferrari racing barchettas of the 1950s. Only 499 SP1 and SP2 combined were built.

The Ferrari Monza SP2, produced from 2019, is a two-seat barchetta from Ferrari's Icona series, sharing the SP1's 6.5-litre V12 producing 799 bhp. The SP2 adds a passenger seat and windscreen, offering a slightly more practical take on the open-top speedster concept.
The Ferrari Amalfi is one of Ferrari's newest models, continuing the brand's tradition of combining cutting-edge technology with Italian design excellence.
The Ferrari 849 Testarossa pays homage to the legendary Testarossa lineage. Available as a coupe and spider, it continues Ferrari's tradition of iconic mid-engined grand tourers.

The Ferrari Luce is Ferrari's first all-electric vehicle, representing a bold new chapter for the marque. It combines Ferrari's design philosophy and performance DNA with zero-emission electric powertrain technology.

The McLaren M6GT, built in 1969, was Bruce McLaren's attempt to create a road-going version of the dominant M6A Can-Am race car. Powered by a Chevrolet 5.0-litre V8, only three examples were completed before Bruce McLaren's tragic death in 1970, making it one of the rarest and most significant McLarens ever built.

The McLaren F1, produced from 1992 to 1998, is one of the most iconic supercars ever built. Designed by Gordon Murray with a central driving position and a BMW-developed 6.1-litre V12 producing 627 bhp, it held the world speed record for a production car for over a decade. Variants include the ultra-rare LM and GT race homologation specials.

The McLaren 12C (originally MP4-12C), produced from 2011 to 2014, was McLaren Automotive's first production road car. Powered by a bespoke 3.8-litre twin-turbo V8 producing up to 625 bhp, it featured a carbon fibre MonoCell chassis and was available as both coupe and spider.

The McLaren P1, produced from 2013 to 2015, is McLaren's first hybrid hypercar and a member of the "Holy Trinity" alongside the LaFerrari and Porsche 918. Combining a 3.8-litre twin-turbo V8 with an electric motor for 903 bhp, only 375 road cars were built. The track-only P1 GTR pushed output to 986 bhp.

The McLaren 650S, produced from 2014 to 2017, replaced the 12C as the core of McLaren's Super Series. With 641 bhp from its 3.8-litre twin-turbo V8 and styling influenced by the P1, it was available as both coupe and spider.

The McLaren 625C, produced from 2015 to 2016, was a detuned version of the 650S designed primarily for Asian markets with softer suspension and a more comfort-oriented setup. With 625 bhp from the same 3.8-litre twin-turbo V8, it offered a more refined grand touring experience.

The McLaren 675LT, produced from 2015 to 2017, was the first modern McLaren to wear the Longtail badge. With 666 bhp and 100kg lighter than the 650S thanks to extensive use of carbon fibre, it delivered sharper, more track-focused performance. Available as coupe and spider, both limited to 500 units each.

The McLaren 540C, produced from 2015 to 2020, was McLaren's most affordable model and the entry point to the Sports Series range. With 533 bhp from the 3.8-litre twin-turbo V8, it offered genuine supercar performance with everyday usability.

The McLaren 570S, produced from 2015 to 2021, was the most popular Sports Series model. With 562 bhp from the 3.8-litre twin-turbo V8 and McLaren's signature carbon fibre MonoCell II chassis, it combined supercar dynamics with daily usability. Available as coupe and spider.

The McLaren 570GT, produced from 2017 to 2021, was the grand touring variant of the Sports Series. Sharing the 570S's 562 bhp twin-turbo V8, it added a glass-roofed touring deck with 220 litres of luggage space, softer suspension, and enhanced sound insulation for long-distance comfort.

The McLaren 600LT, produced from 2018 to 2021, was the second modern Longtail model. With 592 bhp, top-exit exhausts, and 96kg lighter than the 570S, it delivered an uncompromising track-focused driving experience. Available as coupe and spider.

The McLaren 620R, produced from 2019 to 2021, was a road-legal version of the 570S GT4 race car. Limited to 350 units worldwide, it featured 611 bhp, full carbon fibre body panels, and race-derived aerodynamics including a large carbon fibre rear wing. A true road-legal race car.

The McLaren 720S, produced from 2017 to 2023, redefined the Super Series with a new 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 producing 710 bhp and the innovative Monocage II carbon fibre structure. Widely praised for its combination of performance, comfort, and dramatic design, it was available as coupe and spider.

The McLaren 765LT, produced from 2020 to 2023, was the most extreme Longtail yet. With 755 bhp from the 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 and 80kg lighter than the 720S, it delivered devastating track performance. Limited to 765 coupes and 765 spiders, both are highly sought after by collectors.

The McLaren Senna, produced from 2018 to 2020, is the most extreme road-legal McLaren ever built. Named after Ayrton Senna, it produces 789 bhp from its 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 while weighing just 1,198kg dry. Limited to 500 road cars, with a further 75 track-only GTR variants producing even more downforce.

The McLaren GT, produced from 2019 to 2023, was McLaren's first true grand tourer. With 612 bhp from the 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8, a glazed rear luggage bay offering 420 litres of storage, and a focus on long-distance refinement, it aimed to combine supercar performance with genuine GT usability.

The McLaren Speedtail, delivered from 2020, is a three-seat hybrid hyper-GT and the spiritual successor to the F1. With 1,036 bhp from its hybrid V8 powertrain and a top speed of 250 mph, it features a central driving position, teardrop body shape, and flexible carbon fibre rear ailerons. Limited to just 106 examples.

The McLaren Elva, delivered from 2020, is a roofless, windscreen-less open-top roadster inspired by the original Bruce McLaren-Elva race cars of the 1960s. With 804 bhp and weighing just 1,148kg, it features an innovative Active Air Management System that deflects air over the cabin. Limited to 149 examples.

The McLaren Artura, produced from 2022, is McLaren's next-generation hybrid supercar. Featuring an all-new 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 and electric motor producing up to 700 bhp, it rides on the new McLaren Carbon Lightweight Architecture. Available as coupe and spider.

The McLaren 750S, produced from 2023, succeeds the 720S as the core of the Super Series. With 740 bhp from the upgraded 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 and 30kg lighter than its predecessor, it delivers even sharper performance and improved aerodynamics. Available as coupe and spider.

The McLaren GTS, produced from 2024, succeeds the GT as McLaren's grand touring model. With 626 bhp from the 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8, it is lighter and more focused than its predecessor while retaining the practical luggage space and long-distance refinement that defined the GT.
